name: George Washington
bio: George Washington served as the first President of the United States and helped establish the foundations of the federal government. His leadership set precedents for presidential authority, cabinet structure, and the peaceful transfer of power. Washington guided the nation through its earliest challenges and maintained neutrality in foreign conflicts.
platform: Establish a stable federal government, maintain national unity, and promote neutrality in foreign affairs.

name: John Adams
bio: John Adams served as the second President of the United States and played a major role in shaping early American diplomacy. His administration faced rising tensions with France, leading to the Quasi-War, and he prioritized peace despite political pressure. Adams also signed the controversial Alien and Sedition Acts, which remain debated today.
platform: Strengthen national defense, maintain federal authority, and promote stability in foreign relations.

name: Thomas Jefferson
bio: Thomas Jefferson served as the third President of the United States and authored the Declaration of Independence. His presidency is known for the Louisiana Purchase, which doubled the size of the nation, and for efforts to reduce federal government power. Jefferson also faced economic challenges due to trade restrictions and foreign conflicts.
platform: Reduce federal government power, expand the nation westward, and promote agrarian democracy.

name: James Madison
bio: James Madison, known as the 'Father of the Constitution,' served as the fourth President of the United States. His presidency was defined by the War of 1812, a major conflict with Great Britain that tested the young nation's resilience. Madison helped stabilize the country after the war and supported strengthening federal institutions.
platform: Protect American sovereignty, defend national interests, and strengthen federal institutions.

name: James Monroe
bio: James Monroe served as the fifth President of the United States and presided over the 'Era of Good Feelings,' a period of national unity and political calm. His administration is best known for the Monroe Doctrine, a defining statement of American foreign policy. Monroe also oversaw westward expansion and internal improvements.
platform: Promote national unity, support westward expansion, and assert American interests in the Western Hemisphere.

name: John Quincy Adams
bio: John Quincy Adams served as the sixth President of the United States and was known for his strong diplomatic background. His presidency focused on national development, including infrastructure and education, though political opposition limited his agenda. After leaving office, he served in Congress and became a leading voice against slavery.
platform: Promote national infrastructure, expand education, and strengthen American diplomacy.

name: Andrew Jackson
bio: Andrew Jackson served as the seventh President of the United States and was a dominant figure in early American politics. His presidency expanded executive power and introduced populist themes into national governance. Jackson's policies, including the Bank War and Indian Removal Act, had lasting and controversial impacts.
platform: Expand executive authority, oppose the national bank, and promote frontier settlement.

name: Martin Van Buren
bio: Martin Van Buren served as the eighth President of the United States and faced the Panic of 1837 shortly after taking office. His administration struggled with the resulting economic depression, though he supported limited government intervention. Van Buren was a key architect of the Democratic Party and a major figure in early American politics.
platform: Support limited federal government, maintain Democratic Party principles, and stabilize the national economy.

name: William Henry Harrison
bio: William Henry Harrison served as the ninth President of the United States and holds the shortest presidency in American history, dying just one month after taking office. A former military officer and territorial governor, Harrison was a prominent figure in early frontier conflicts. His death raised major constitutional questions about presidential succession.
platform: Promote Whig economic policies and restore executive-legislative balance.

name: John Tyler
bio: John Tyler became the tenth President of the United States after the death of William Henry Harrison, establishing the precedent that a vice president fully assumes the presidency. His administration clashed with Whig leadership, leading to his expulsion from the party. Tyler supported states' rights and oversaw the annexation of Texas.
platform: Support states' rights, limit federal authority, and promote territorial expansion.

name: James K. Polk
bio: James K. Polk served as the eleventh President of the United States and is known for overseeing the largest territorial expansion in American history. His administration led the nation through the Mexican-American War, resulting in vast new western lands. Polk also established the Independent Treasury and fulfilled nearly all of his major campaign promises.
education: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
platform: Annex Texas, settle the Oregon boundary dispute, and expand U.S. territory westward.

name: Zachary Taylor
bio: Zachary Taylor served as the twelfth President of the United States and was a celebrated military hero before entering politics. His presidency was cut short by his sudden death after only sixteen months in office. Taylor opposed the expansion of slavery into new territories, a stance that placed him at odds with Southern leaders.
platform: Preserve the Union and oppose the expansion of slavery into western territories.

name: Millard Fillmore
bio: Millard Fillmore became the thirteenth President of the United States after the death of Zachary Taylor. His presidency is most associated with the Compromise of 1850, which attempted to ease tensions between free and slave states. Fillmore supported enforcement of the Fugitive Slave Act, a decision that damaged his political standing.
platform: Promote sectional compromise and preserve the Union amid rising tensions over slavery.

name: Franklin Pierce
bio: Franklin Pierce served as the fourteenth President of the United States during a period of growing sectional conflict. His support for the Kansas-Nebraska Act intensified national divisions over slavery and contributed to the collapse of the Whig Party. Pierce struggled to maintain unity as the nation moved closer to civil war.
platform: Promote national expansion and support popular sovereignty in new territories.

name: James Buchanan
bio: James Buchanan served as the fifteenth President of the United States and presided over the nation during the final years before the Civil War. His inability to resolve the escalating conflict between free and slave states weakened the Union. Buchanan believed secession was illegal but also believed the federal government lacked authority to stop it.
platform: Preserve the Union through compromise and maintain peace between free and slave states.

name: Abraham Lincoln
bio: Abraham Lincoln served as the sixteenth President of the United States and led the nation through the Civil War, its greatest constitutional and moral crisis. He preserved the Union, issued the Emancipation Proclamation, and promoted the passage of the Thirteenth Amendment. Lincoln’s leadership reshaped the nation and remains central to American history.
platform: Preserve the Union, prevent the expansion of slavery, and strengthen federal authority.

name: Andrew Johnson
bio: Andrew Johnson became the seventeenth President of the United States following the assassination of Abraham Lincoln. His lenient Reconstruction policies toward the former Confederate states brought him into conflict with Congress. Johnson was impeached by the House but acquitted in the Senate by one vote, remaining in office.
platform: Implement lenient Reconstruction policies and restore Southern states to the Union quickly.

name: Ulysses S. Grant
bio: Ulysses S. Grant served as the eighteenth President of the United States after leading Union forces to victory in the Civil War. His presidency focused on Reconstruction and civil rights for freed slaves, though it was marred by corruption scandals involving members of his administration. Grant remained personally popular and respected for his wartime leadership.
education: United States Military Academy at West Point
platform: Support Reconstruction, protect civil rights, and stabilize the postwar nation.

name: Rutherford B. Hayes
bio: Rutherford B. Hayes served as the nineteenth President of the United States after winning one of the most disputed elections in American history. His presidency marked the end of Reconstruction as federal troops were withdrawn from the South. Hayes promoted civil service reform and worked to restore trust in government institutions.
platform: Promote civil service reform and restore national unity after Reconstruction.

name: James A. Garfield
bio: James A. Garfield served as the twentieth President of the United States, though his time in office was tragically brief due to his assassination. Garfield advocated for civil service reform and sought to reduce corruption in government. His death accelerated national support for major reforms in federal hiring practices.
platform: Advance civil service reform and reduce corruption in federal government.

name: Chester A. Arthur
bio: Chester A. Arthur became the twenty-first President of the United States after the assassination of James A. Garfield. Despite his background in machine politics, Arthur surprised many by championing civil service reform, including signing the Pendleton Act. His presidency helped modernize the federal government and reduce patronage corruption.
platform: Promote civil service reform and modernize federal administration.

name: Grover Cleveland
bio: Grover Cleveland served as the twenty-second President of the United States and was known for his commitment to honesty and fiscal conservatism. He opposed political corruption and fought against excessive government spending. Cleveland also confronted major labor unrest and economic challenges during his first term.
platform: Reduce government corruption, limit federal spending, and uphold the gold standard.

name: Benjamin Harrison
bio: Benjamin Harrison served as the twenty-third President of the United States and oversaw significant economic legislation, including the Sherman Antitrust Act. His administration supported high tariffs and expanded the Navy. Harrison also admitted six new states to the Union, the most under any president since Washington.
platform: Support high tariffs, strengthen the Navy, and regulate monopolies.

name: Grover Cleveland
bio: Grover Cleveland returned as the twenty-fourth President of the United States, becoming the only president to serve nonconsecutive terms. His second term was dominated by the Panic of 1893, one of the worst economic crises in U.S. history. Cleveland defended the gold standard and worked to stabilize the economy amid widespread hardship.
platform: Defend the gold standard, reduce government spending, and restore economic stability.

name: William McKinley
bio: William McKinley served as the twenty-fifth President of the United States and led the nation during the Spanish-American War, which resulted in significant territorial gains. His presidency oversaw rapid economic growth and the rise of American global influence. McKinley was assassinated in 1901, leading to Theodore Roosevelt’s succession.
platform: Support protective tariffs, promote economic growth, and expand American influence abroad.

name: Theodore Roosevelt
bio: Theodore Roosevelt served as the twenty-sixth President of the United States and transformed the presidency into a more activist and globally engaged office. He championed progressive reforms, trust-busting, and conservation, establishing national parks and wildlife refuges. Roosevelt also expanded American influence abroad through his 'Big Stick' diplomacy.
platform: Promote progressive reforms, regulate large corporations, and expand conservation efforts.

name: William Howard Taft
bio: William Howard Taft served as the twenty-seventh President of the United States and later became Chief Justice of the Supreme Court, the only person to hold both offices. His presidency focused on trust-busting and civil service reform, though he struggled to unite the Republican Party. Taft favored judicial solutions over political confrontation.
education: Yale University; University of Cincinnati College of Law
platform: Continue antitrust enforcement, strengthen civil service, and promote judicial efficiency.

name: Woodrow Wilson
bio: Woodrow Wilson served as the twenty-eighth President of the United States and led the nation through World War I. His administration enacted major progressive reforms, including the Federal Reserve Act and antitrust legislation. Wilson championed the League of Nations, though the U.S. ultimately did not join due to Senate opposition.
education: Princeton University; University of Virginia School of Law; Johns Hopkins University
platform: Advance progressive reforms, regulate the economy, and promote international cooperation.

name: Warren G. Harding
bio: Warren G. Harding served as the twenty-ninth President of the United States and promised a 'return to normalcy' after World War I. His administration focused on economic recovery and limited government intervention, but it was later marred by major scandals, including the Teapot Dome affair. Harding died in office in 1923.
platform: Promote economic recovery, reduce government intervention, and restore national stability.

name: Calvin Coolidge
bio: Calvin Coolidge served as the thirtieth President of the United States and became known for his quiet demeanor and belief in limited government. His administration oversaw strong economic growth during the Roaring Twenties. Coolidge supported tax cuts, reduced federal spending, and maintained a pro-business approach to governance.
platform: Limit federal government, reduce taxes, and support business-friendly policies.

name: Herbert Hoover
bio: Herbert Hoover served as the thirty-first President of the United States and entered office just months before the stock market crash of 1929. His administration struggled to respond effectively to the Great Depression, though he supported public works projects like the Hoover Dam. Hoover’s belief in limited government shaped his cautious approach to economic relief.
platform: Promote economic efficiency, support limited government, and encourage private-sector solutions.

name: Franklin D. Roosevelt
bio: Franklin D. Roosevelt served as the thirty-second President of the United States and led the nation through the Great Depression and World War II. His New Deal programs reshaped the federal government’s role in the economy, providing relief, recovery, and reform. Roosevelt remains the only president elected to four terms and is one of the most influential leaders in American history.
education: Harvard University; Columbia Law School (did not graduate)
platform: Implement New Deal reforms, provide economic relief, and lead the nation through global conflict.

name: Harry S. Truman
bio: Harry S. Truman became the thirty-third President of the United States after the death of Franklin D. Roosevelt. He made the decision to use atomic weapons to end World War II and later led the nation through the early Cold War. Truman implemented the Marshall Plan, supported the creation of NATO, and advanced civil rights initiatives.
platform: Support postwar recovery, contain Soviet influence, and promote civil rights.

name: Dwight D. Eisenhower
bio: Dwight D. Eisenhower served as the thirty-fourth President of the United States after commanding Allied forces in Europe during World War II. His presidency oversaw economic prosperity, the creation of the Interstate Highway System, and the early years of the Civil Rights Movement. Eisenhower emphasized moderation and stability in domestic and foreign policy.
education: United States Military Academy at West Point
platform: Promote national infrastructure, maintain global stability, and support moderate governance.

name: John F. Kennedy
bio: John F. Kennedy served as the thirty-fifth President of the United States and inspired a new generation with his call to public service. His presidency confronted major Cold War crises, including the Bay of Pigs invasion and the Cuban Missile Crisis. Kennedy also advanced civil rights and launched the effort to land a man on the Moon.
platform: Promote civil rights, strengthen national defense, and advance scientific progress.

name: Lyndon B. Johnson
bio: Lyndon B. Johnson became the thirty-sixth President of the United States after the assassination of John F. Kennedy. He championed the Great Society, a sweeping set of domestic programs aimed at reducing poverty and expanding civil rights. Johnson also escalated U.S. involvement in the Vietnam War, which overshadowed many of his domestic achievements.
platform: Advance civil rights, reduce poverty, and expand federal social programs.

name: Richard Nixon
bio: Richard Nixon served as the thirty-seventh President of the United States and is known for his foreign policy achievements, including opening relations with China and negotiating arms control agreements with the Soviet Union. His presidency ended in resignation due to the Watergate scandal, making him the only U.S. president to resign from office.
education: Whittier College; Duke University School of Law
platform: Restore law and order, pursue détente with major powers, and reduce federal intervention.

name: Gerald Ford
bio: Gerald Ford became the thirty-eighth President of the United States following Richard Nixon’s resignation. He sought to restore trust in government and issued a controversial pardon of Nixon. Ford faced economic challenges, including inflation and recession, and worked to stabilize the nation during a period of political uncertainty.
education: University of Michigan; Yale Law School
platform: Restore public trust, address inflation, and promote government transparency.

name: Jimmy Carter
bio: Jimmy Carter served as the thirty-ninth President of the United States and emphasized human rights, energy reform, and government integrity. His presidency faced major challenges, including inflation, an energy crisis, and the Iran hostage situation. Carter later became known for his extensive humanitarian work after leaving office.
platform: Promote human rights, reform energy policy, and restore trust in government.

name: Ronald Reagan
bio: Ronald Reagan served as the fortieth President of the United States and ushered in a conservative political shift known as the Reagan Revolution. His administration emphasized tax cuts, deregulation, and a strong national defense. Reagan played a major role in ending the Cold War and remains a defining figure in modern American politics.
platform: Reduce taxes, limit government regulation, and strengthen national defense.

name: George H. W. Bush
bio: George H. W. Bush served as the forty-first President of the United States and oversaw the end of the Cold War and the successful Gulf War coalition. His administration managed major global transitions as the Soviet Union collapsed. Domestically, Bush faced economic challenges that contributed to his reelection defeat.
platform: Promote global stability, support free trade, and maintain strong national defense.

name: Bill Clinton
bio: Bill Clinton served as the forty-second President of the United States and presided over strong economic growth, welfare reform, and a balanced federal budget. His administration expanded global trade and pursued diplomatic engagement abroad. Clinton’s presidency was also marked by impeachment proceedings, though he was acquitted by the Senate.
education: Georgetown University; University of Oxford (Rhodes Scholar); Yale Law School
platform: Promote economic growth, expand global trade, and reform welfare programs.

name: George W. Bush
bio: George W. Bush served as the forty-third President of the United States and led the nation through the aftermath of the September 11 attacks. His administration launched the War on Terror, initiated wars in Afghanistan and Iraq, and enacted major tax cuts. Bush also faced significant domestic challenges, including Hurricane Katrina and the 2008 financial crisis.
platform: Strengthen national security, reduce taxes, and promote education reform.

name: Barack Obama
bio: Barack Obama served as the forty-fourth President of the United States and was the first African American to hold the office. His administration enacted the Affordable Care Act, oversaw the recovery from the Great Recession, and advanced major environmental and foreign policy initiatives. Obama emphasized diplomacy and multilateral cooperation.
education: Occidental College; Columbia University; Harvard Law School
platform: Expand healthcare access, promote clean energy, and strengthen international alliances.

name: Donald Trump
bio: Donald Trump served as the forty-fifth President of the United States and pursued an agenda focused on deregulation, tax cuts, and reshaping trade policy. His administration appointed three Supreme Court justices and implemented major shifts in immigration and foreign policy. Trump’s presidency was marked by intense political polarization and two impeachment trials.
platform: Reduce regulation, renegotiate trade agreements, and strengthen border security.

name: Joe Biden
bio: Joe Biden served as the forty-sixth President of the United States and focused on pandemic recovery, infrastructure investment, and strengthening international alliances. His administration passed major legislation on infrastructure, climate, and economic relief. Biden emphasized restoring political norms and expanding federal support for families and workers.
education: University of Delaware; Syracuse University College of Law
platform: Expand healthcare access, invest in infrastructure, and strengthen global alliances.
